findings.title: FTP banner discloses product identity
findings.description: FTP banner: 220 MikroTik FTP server (MikroTik 7.20.8) ready

findings.title: No FTP brute force protection
findings.description: 10 rapid failed login attempts completed without lockout or delay. Brute force attacks are feasible.

findings.title: FTP service DoS: persistent degradation after attack traffic
findings.description: After receiving ~223 FTP attack tests (including 65536-byte command overflows and rapid connection cycling), the FTP service entered a permanent degraded state where it accepts TCP connections but returns empty banners and drops all commands. Disabling/re-enabling the service via /ip/service does not recover it. A full router reboot is required to restore FTP functionality. This constitutes a persistent denial of service against the FTP service.

evidence.trigger: attack_ftpd.py Section 2 (65536-byte overflows) and Section 8 (rapid connect/disconnect cycling)
evidence.symptom: FTP accepts TCP connections but returns empty banner, all commands fail
evidence.recovery: disable/enable FTP service does NOT fix it, full router reboot required

findings.title: FTP bounce attack: PORT/EPRT accepts loopback and internal addresses
findings.description: The MikroTik FTP server accepts PORT and EPRT commands targeting loopback (127.0.0.1, ::1), 0.0.0.0, and internal network addresses. This enables FTP bounce attacks to scan internal services or relay data connections through the router. Both IPv4 (PORT) and IPv6 (EPRT) variants are affected.

findings.title: FTP REST accepts negative and out-of-range offsets
findings.description: The FTP server accepts REST (restart) offsets of -1, 4294967295 (UINT32_MAX), and 99999999999999999 without validation. This could lead to integer overflow conditions during subsequent RETR/STOR operations on the 32-bit binary.
